annotations.label: Mathematics

anchor.snippet: Mathematics is a field of knowledge concerned with abstract concepts

annotations.note: An informal description of the discipline, not a formalizable mathematical object.

annotations.label: Number theory

anchor.snippet: Number theory evolved from the manipulation of numbers

annotations.note: A description of the subject area rather than a single formal object.

annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem

anchor.snippet: A prominent example is Fermat's Last Theorem

annotations.note: `FermatLastTheorem` is defined exactly as the statement that aⁿ+bⁿ=cⁿ has no positive solutions for n≥3.

annotations.label: Goldbach's conjecture

anchor.snippet: every even integer greater than 2 is the sum of two prime numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib's only 'Goldbach' result concerns coprimality of Fermat numbers, not the even-sum-of-two-primes conjecture.

annotations.label: Geometry

anchor.snippet: Geometry is one of the oldest branches of mathematics

annotations.note: A description of the discipline rather than a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: The resulting Euclidean geometry is the study of shapes and their arrangements

annotations.note: The setting `EuclideanSpace` and the `EuclideanGeometry` namespace formalize the objects, but the informal disciplinary definition itself is not a statement.

annotations.label: Non-Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: mathematicians discovered non-Euclidean geometries , which do not follow the parallel postulate

annotations.note: No formalization of non-Euclidean (hyperbolic/elliptic) geometry as such was found.

annotations.label: Projective geometry

anchor.snippet: extends Euclidean geometry by adding points at infinity

annotations.note: `Projectivization` formalizes projective space, but the disciplinary description is not itself a statement.

annotations.label: Affine geometry

anchor.snippet: the study of properties relative to parallelism and independent from the concept of length

annotations.note: `AffineSpace` (notation for `AddTorsor`) formalizes the central object, not the informal disciplinary definition.

annotations.label: Differential geometry

anchor.snippet: the study of curves, surfaces, and their generalizations, which are defined using differentiable functions

annotations.note: Smooth manifolds via `IsManifold` formalize the objects studied, but the disciplinary description is not a statement.

annotations.label: Manifold theory

anchor.snippet: the study of shapes that are not necessarily embedded in a larger space

annotations.note: `ChartedSpace` formalizes abstract manifolds, but the disciplinary description itself is not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Riemannian geometry

anchor.snippet: the study of distance properties in curved spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib has developing Riemannian-geometry infrastructure (`Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.Riemannian`), but the disciplinary description is not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Algebraic geometry

anchor.snippet: the study of curves, surfaces, and their generalizations, which are defined using polynomials

annotations.note: `Scheme` formalizes the central object of modern algebraic geometry, but the disciplinary description is not a statement.

annotations.label: Topology

anchor.snippet: the study of properties that are kept under continuous deformations

annotations.note: `TopologicalSpace` formalizes the central object, but the informal disciplinary definition is not itself a statement.

annotations.label: Discrete geometry

anchor.snippet: the study of finite configurations in geometry

annotations.note: No object corresponding to the discipline of discrete geometry was found.

annotations.label: Convex geometry

anchor.snippet: the study of convex sets , which takes its importance from its applications in optimization

annotations.note: `Convex` formalizes convex sets, but the disciplinary description is not itself a statement.

annotations.label: Complex geometry

anchor.snippet: the geometry obtained by replacing real numbers with complex numbers

annotations.note: No formalization of complex geometry as a discipline/object was found.

annotations.label: Algebra

anchor.snippet: Algebra is the art of manipulating equations and formulas

annotations.note: A description of the discipline (distinct from the `Algebra` typeclass, which means an algebra over a ring).

annotations.label: Algebraic structure

anchor.snippet: The concept of algebraic structure addresses this, consisting of a set whose elements are unspecified

annotations.note: Mathlib has many specific structures but no single unified 'algebraic structure' object.

annotations.label: Vector spaces

anchor.snippet: whose study is essentially the same as linear algebra

annotations.note: A vector space is exactly `Module K V` over a field K; Mathlib's `Module` generalizes this to semirings.

annotations.label: Commutative algebra

anchor.snippet: which is the study of commutative rings

annotations.note: `CommRing` formalizes the central object (commutative rings), but the discipline itself is not a statement.

annotations.label: Boolean algebra

anchor.snippet: which is widely used for the study of the logical structure of computers

annotations.note: The `BooleanAlgebra` class directly formalizes Boolean algebras.

annotations.label: Universal algebra and category theory

anchor.snippet: The study of types of algebraic structures as mathematical objects is the purpose of universal algebra and category theory

annotations.note: Category theory is formalized via `CategoryTheory.Category`, but universal algebra has no general formalization.

annotations.label: Calculus

anchor.snippet: It is fundamentally the study of the relationship between variables that depend continuously on each other

annotations.note: Differentiation (`deriv`/`fderiv`) and integration are formalized, but the disciplinary description is not itself a statement.

annotations.label: Real and complex analysis

anchor.snippet: Analysis is further subdivided into real analysis , where variables represent real numbers

annotations.note: Real and complex analysis are extensively developed under `Mathlib.Analysis`, but no single object defines the discipline.

annotations.label: Functional analysis

anchor.snippet: where variables represent varying functions

annotations.note: Normed/Banach/Hilbert space theory (e.g. `NormedSpace`) formalizes the setting, but the discipline itself is not a statement.

annotations.label: Numerical analysis

anchor.snippet: mainly devoted to the computation of solutions of ordinary and partial differential equations

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize numerical analysis of ODEs/PDEs.

annotations.label: Discrete mathematics

anchor.snippet: Discrete mathematics, broadly speaking, is the study of individual, countable mathematical objects

annotations.note: A description of the subject area, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Four color theorem

anchor.snippet: The four color theorem and optimal sphere packing were two major problems

annotations.note: No formalization of the four color theorem exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P versus NP problem

anchor.snippet: The P versus NP problem , which remains open to this day

annotations.note: Computational complexity classes P and NP and their (in)equality are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Combinatorics

anchor.snippet: the art of enumerating mathematical objects that satisfy some given constraints

annotations.note: Combinatorics is extensively developed but has no single defining object matching the disciplinary description.

annotations.label: Cantor's diagonal argument

anchor.snippet: by showing that this implies different sizes of infinity

annotations.note: `Function.cantor_surjective` (with `cantor_injective`) is exactly Cantor's diagonal argument that no function α→Set α is surjective.

annotations.label: Peano axioms for natural numbers

anchor.snippet: in Peano arithmetic , the natural numbers are defined by

annotations.note: ℕ realizes the Peano axioms (e.g. `Nat.succ_injective`, `Nat.succ_ne_zero`, induction via `Nat.rec`), but they are not bundled as an explicit axiomatization.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness theorems

anchor.snippet: in every consistent formal system that contains the natural numbers, there are theorems that are true

annotations.note: Only the Gödel beta function helper (`Mathlib.Logic.Godel.GodelBetaFunction`) is present, not the incompleteness theorems.

annotations.label: Computational mathematics

anchor.snippet: Computational mathematics is the study of mathematical problems that are typically too large for human, numerical capacity

annotations.note: A description of the discipline, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Axiom / postulate

anchor.snippet: An axiom or postulate is a mathematical statement that is taken to be true without need of proof

annotations.note: A metamathematical/terminological notion, not a formalizable mathematical object.

annotations.label: Conjecture

anchor.snippet: If a mathematical statement has yet to be proven (or disproven), it is termed a conjecture

annotations.note: A terminological notion about proof status, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Theorem

anchor.snippet: a statement that is proven to be true becomes a theorem

annotations.note: A metamathematical/terminological notion, not a formalizable object.

annotations.label: Lemma

anchor.snippet: A specialized theorem that is mainly used to prove another theorem is called a lemma

annotations.note: A terminological notion, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Corollary

anchor.snippet: A proven instance that forms part of a more general finding is termed a corollary

annotations.note: A terminological notion, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Every free module is flat / a field is a ring

anchor.snippet: every free module is flat

annotations.note: `Module.Flat.of_free` directly states that free modules are flat.

annotations.label: Prime factorization and RSA cryptosystem

anchor.snippet: A notable example is the prime factorization of natural numbers

annotations.note: Prime factorization is formalized (`Nat.primeFactorsList`, unique factorization), but the RSA cryptosystem is not.

annotations.label: Ellipses as planetary orbits

anchor.snippet: A second historical example is the theory of ellipses

annotations.note: Kepler orbits / planetary ellipses are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetry group

anchor.snippet: the symmetries of an object form a group known as the symmetry group

annotations.note: Symmetry/automorphism groups are realized via `Equiv.Perm` and `MulAut`, but there is no single general 'symmetry group of an object' construction.

annotations.label: Apéry's theorem

anchor.snippet: An extreme example is Apery's theorem

annotations.note: The irrationality of ζ(3) is listed as a target but not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinitude of primes

anchor.snippet: Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many prime numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.exists_infinite_primes` is Euclid's theorem that there exist arbitrarily large primes.
